title: Subject Pronouns

primarySong.title: Soy Yo
primarySong.note: Replaced 'Yo Perreo Sola' — explicit-content swap. The chorus 'soy yo' is the lesson's pronoun anchor.

notes: Module 5 audio set — narration-first per Reine's vision (locked 2026-05-15). Each cue is an instructive mini-narration written by the content-builder in module-005-content.ts.draft (audioTextManifest section), not a single-word TTS. Scripts are mixed-language by design — accept MVP nova-pronunciation tradeoffs on the Spanish tokens embedded in English narration. One file per cue, no slow/normal pairs (per Reine, 2026-05-17). 10 cues + 1 conditional reggaeton variant = 11 files total. Cues map 1:1 to UX play buttons via uiAttachmentPoint values listed in the draft.

ttsTriggers.text: Today we're learning the subject pronouns — the small words that name who is doing the doing. Yo, tú, él, ella, nosotros, ellos. As you listen to 'Soy Yo' by Bomba Estéreo, count how often the singer says yo. Every time she does, she's choosing to. Press listen when you're ready.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, teacherly — sets up the lesson, primes the song
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Mixed-language opener. nova will read English as American English and Spanish tokens (yo, tú, él, ella, nosotros, ellos) with Spanish phonemes — this is the expected MVP behavior. Watch the stressed 'tú' (ú accent) and the 'll' in ella (Latin-American soft y, not Castilian ly). 'Soy Yo' title should sound like the song title, not 'soy-why-oh' — listen for clean Spanish pronunciation of both words. Estimated duration: 24s.

ttsTriggers.text: 'Yo' means 'I'. A pure 'yoh' sound — short and open. And here's the trick: Spanish often drops the pronoun, so when a singer says yo out loud, it's for emphasis. Me, specifically.
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: The 'yo' tokens (3 occurrences) must sound like Spanish yo — soft Latin-American Y onset (closer to 'yoh' than English 'joh'). The literal 'yoh' phonetic spelling in the script gives nova a hint but does not force it; QA listen to the first 'Yo' after 'means' to confirm. Estimated duration: 13s.

ttsTriggers.text: 'Tú' means 'you' — the informal singular. Use it with friends, peers, kids, people your age. The accent mark on the ú is what tells it apart from tu, which means 'your'. Same sound, different jobs.
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: MOST IMPORTANT QA ITEM IN THE MODULE. The script intentionally says 'same sound, different jobs' — so tú and tu MUST sound identical in this cue (both as Spanish 'too'). The accent in tú does NOT change the sound itself in modern Latin-American Spanish, it just marks stress for disambiguation. If nova accidentally Anglicizes 'tu' to 'tuh' the lesson's teaching point breaks. Listen carefully and regenerate if there's any phonetic split between the two tokens. Estimated duration: 14s.

ttsTriggers.text: 'Él' means 'he' — with an accent on the é to tell it apart from el, which means 'the'. 'Ella' means 'she' — eh-yah. Together they cover any single third person you're talking about.
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Like tu/tú, the script explicitly teaches that él and el sound the same — the accent on é is for disambiguation, not phonetic change. Both tokens must be 'el' /el/. The 'll' in ella must render as the Latin-American soft 'y' sound — the phonetic hint 'eh-yah' in the script helps nova lock this in. QA listen for any Castilian 'ly' drift on ella. Estimated duration: 14s.

ttsTriggers.text: 'Nosotros' means 'we' — any group that includes you. If the group is all female, switch to 'nosotras'. Mixed or unknown? Stay with nosotros. It's the default.
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Stress on the middle syllable of all four occurrences of nosotros/nosotras: no-SO-tros / no-SO-tras. The masculine/feminine vowel contrast (final o vs final a) must be crisp so the teaching distinction lands. Estimated duration: 12s.

ttsTriggers.text: 'Ellos' means 'they' — a masculine or mixed group. 'Ellas' is the all-female version. Same logic as nosotros and nosotras. The default for unknown groups is the masculine form.
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: The 'll' in both ellos and ellas must be Latin-American soft 'y' (eh-yohs / eh-yahs), NOT Castilian 'ly'. Stress on the first syllable: E-llos / E-llas. The final o/a vowel contrast is the gender teaching moment — both must be crisp. Same QA risk as ella in the previous cue. Estimated duration: 12s.

ttsTriggers.text: 'Yo soy de México.' I am from Mexico. Fill in your own country or city. Soy means 'I am' — you'll meet the full verb in Module 9. For now, just memorize the whole chunk: yo soy de, plus a place. The single most useful sentence you'll leave today's lesson with.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, conversational — modeling a real sentence the learner can use
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: The opening 'Yo soy de México.' is the learner's takeaway sentence — it MUST sound natural, like a real self-introduction. The 'é' in México is the Latin-American 'eh' (not 'aye' as in some English mispronunciations). The repeated 'yo soy de' near the end should sound consistent with the opening to anchor the memorization. Estimated duration: 17s.

ttsTriggers.text: Try this with me. Point at yourself and say 'yo'. Point at a friend your age and say 'tú'. Now one full sentence — 'Yo soy de' — and add your country. Say it out loud. Your mouth needs the reps, not just your eyes.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, encouraging — coaching the learner to actually speak
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: The instructional rhythm matters: there should be a clear pause after each 'and say X' so the learner has space to actually point and speak. 'Yo' and 'tú' must be cleanly Spanish-pronounced. 'Yo soy de' should match the phrase-chunk cue's pronunciation for consistency. Estimated duration: 16s.

ttsTriggers.text: Bomba Estéreo formed in Bogotá in 2005, but their sound was born on Colombia's Caribbean coast — the cumbia rhythms, the chants, the salt air. Their lead singer, Li Saumet, grew up in Santa Marta. When the band released 'Soy Yo' in 2015, the title was already the whole message. Soy yo — I am me. The song became an anthem for kids who didn't fit in, with a music video starring an eleven-year-old girl walking through her neighborhood being unapologetically herself. Listen for how often Li sings yo. Spanish usually drops the subject pronoun, so when a singer chooses to say yo out loud, again and again, it's a deliberate choice. It means: me, specifically, no one else. Every time you hear yo in the chorus, you're hearing a Spanish speaker plant a flag.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, reflective — narration tone for the artist/cultural bio
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Longest cue in the module (~52s at 0.95x). Spanish proper nouns must be pronounced as Spanish: Bomba Estéreo (BOM-ba es-TE-re-o, accent on É), Bogotá (bo-go-TÁ, stressed final syllable), Li Saumet (Lee sow-MET — French/Colombian surname), Santa Marta, Soy Yo. Slightly slowed speed (0.95) gives the learner room to process the longer narrative. QA listen specifically to: (1) Bogotá stress, (2) the two repetitions of 'soy yo' inside the narration, (3) all 'yo' occurrences near the end where the script emphasizes 'plant a flag.' Estimated duration: 52s.

ttsTriggers.text: If you picked reggaeton, your song today is 'Yo Quiero Bailar' by Ivy Queen — La Reina del Reggaetón, the Queen of Reggaeton. She was making this music in the early 2000s, years before Bad Bunny brought the genre to global pop charts. Listen to the chorus: she opens with yo, then pivots to tú — drawing a line between what she wants and what someone else assumes. Same emphatic-pronoun energy as 'Soy Yo,' just a generation earlier.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, reflective — narration tone, pair-toned with the reading passage
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: CONDITIONAL cue — only plays when user.genre === 'reggaeton'. Spanish-token QA: 'Yo Quiero Bailar' (yo kee-EH-ro buy-LAR), 'Ivy Queen' (English pronunciation — her stage name is English), 'La Reina del Reggaetón' (la REY-na del rreg-gae-TÓN — final stressed syllable, soft single-tap R on Reina, doubled R on Reggaetón is fine if rolled). 'Bad Bunny' English. 'Soy Yo' Spanish. Stretch goal — generate if budget permits, otherwise defer to a later batch. Estimated duration: 28s.

ttsTriggers.text: You can now name who is doing the doing. Five pronouns — yo, tú, él, ella, nosotros, ellos. And you know the secret: Spanish drops the pronoun by default, so when a speaker says it, they mean it. Try one sentence out loud tomorrow — 'Yo soy de' plus your country. Say it like you mean it.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, celebratory — closing the loop, looking forward
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Closing cue — should feel like a coach giving a final pep talk, not a reading. The five-pronoun roll-call (yo, tú, él, ella, nosotros, ellos) should be paced naturally with brief beats between pronouns, not rushed. 'Yo soy de' must match the phrase-chunk cue's pronunciation. The final 'Say it like you mean it' lands the lesson — listen for a natural cadence, not flat narration. Estimated duration: 18s.
